docs.title: Concept Taxonomy
docs.text: This taxonomy organizes the 350 concepts into 14 categories for the Interactive Infographics for Intelligent Textbooks learning graph.

docs.title: FOUND — Foundation Concepts
docs.text: Core definitions and foundational ideas that underpin the entire course. Includes infographic types, intelligent textbooks, MicroSims, and basic interaction concepts.

docs.title: TAXON — Infographic Taxonomy
docs.text: Classification systems for organizing infographic types by purpose, format, complexity, medium, and audience.

docs.title: SLIDE — Slide Art Diagrams
docs.text: Specific diagram types from presentation tools including list, process, cycle, hierarchy, relationship, pyramid, matrix, and picture diagram types.

docs.title: FRAME — Visual Frameworks
docs.text: Visual problem-solving frameworks including Dan Roam's Back of the Napkin, the Six W's, and the SQVID decision framework.

docs.title: CAUSE — Causal Loop Diagrams
docs.text: Systems thinking concepts including causal loop diagrams, feedback loops, polarity, reinforcing/balancing loops, and leverage points.

docs.title: OVRLY — Overlayment Patterns
docs.text: The four interactive overlay patterns (rectangular, polygon, callout-to-edge, floating label) and their editing, configuration, and JSON file formats.

docs.title: JSLIB — JavaScript Libraries
docs.text: JavaScript visualization libraries (p5.js, D3.js, Chart.js, vis-network, Leaflet) and their specific features, functions, and chart types.

docs.title: WEBFD — Web Fundamentals
docs.text: Core web technologies including HTML, CSS, JavaScript fundamentals, JSON, SVG, Canvas, DOM manipulation, and responsive design.

docs.title: MSIM — MicroSim Standards
docs.text: MicroSim packaging conventions, directory structure, metadata, quality scoring, iframe embedding, and standardization checklist.

docs.title: GENAI — Generative AI
docs.text: AI-assisted infographic creation including text-to-image generation, overlay generation, code generation, prompt engineering, and quality review.

docs.title: TRACK — Tracking and Analytics
docs.text: Student interaction tracking, xAPI, event logging, engagement metrics, learning analytics, and instructor dashboards.

docs.title: LEARN — Learning Science
docs.text: Educational theory including cognitive load, dual coding, multimedia learning, Bloom's Taxonomy, scaffolding, and assessment.

docs.title: DEPLY — Deployment and Tools
docs.text: MkDocs, GitHub Pages, Git version control, CI/CD, Markdown, site configuration, and deployment workflows.

docs.title: ADVIZ — Advanced Visualization
docs.text: Advanced diagram types, interaction patterns, design patterns, accessibility, and specialized visualization techniques.

docs.title: Scoring Rubric Results
docs.text: Element Max Points Score Notes Title 5 5 Clear, descriptive: "Interactive Infographics for Intelligent Textbooks" Target Audience 5 5 Instructional designers + 5 specific professional roles Prerequisites 5 5 Four prerequisites clearly listed with appropriate level Main Topics Covered 10 10 12 comprehensive topics with detailed subtopics Topics Excluded 5 5 11 out-of-scope areas clearly described with rationale Learning Outcomes Header 5 5 Clear Bloom's Taxonomy (2001 Revised) structure Remember Level 10 10 5 specific, actionable outcomes with action verbs Understand Level 10 10 5 specific, actionable outcomes with action verbs Apply Level 10 10 5 specific, actionable outcomes with action verbs Analyze Level 10 10 5 specific, actionable outcomes with action verbs Evaluate Level 10 10 5 specific, actionable outcomes with action verbs Create Level 10 10 5 specific outcomes including capstone portfolio project Descriptive Context 5 5 Overview provides context about AI-assisted creation and MicroSim standards Total 100 100

docs.title: Strengths
docs.text:
• Exceptionally well-structured with all 12 topic areas containing detailed subtopics
  • All six Bloom's Taxonomy levels represented with 5 outcomes each (30 total)
  • Clear audience targeting with multiple professional roles
  • Comprehensive exclusions list that sets clear course boundaries
  • Strong integration of MicroSim standards throughout
  • Practical focus on deployable artifacts (MicroSim packages, overlay.json, metadata.json)

docs.title: Percy the Peacock — Mascot Style Guide
docs.text: This page shows all seven admonition styles for Percy the Peacock, the pedagogical mascot for Interactive Infographics. Use it as a reference when writing chapter content.

docs.title: Welcome (Chapter Openings)
docs.text:
Use mascot-welcome at the start of each chapter to introduce the topic.

Let's Make It Visual!

Welcome to interactive infographics! I'm Percy, your guide through the world of data visualization and interactive design. Whether we're building overlay hotspots or crafting causal loop diagrams, I'll be right here with you. Let's make it visual!

docs.title: Thinking (Key Concepts)
docs.text:
Use mascot-thinking when introducing a major concept students must deeply understand.

Key Insight

Notice the pattern here: every interactive infographic follows the same architecture — a base image, an overlay configuration in JSON, and a JavaScript event handler. Once you understand this three-part pattern, you can build any type of infographic.

docs.title: Tip (Hints and Strategies)
docs.text:
Use mascot-tip for design strategies, workflow shortcuts, or best practices.

Percy's Tip

Always test your infographics at both 600px and 1400px widths to ensure they're truly responsive. The aliceblue drawing region should scale smoothly without breaking your layout or cutting off content.

docs.title: Warning (Common Mistakes)
docs.text:
Use mascot-warning to flag misconceptions or frequent errors.

Common Mistake

Don't forget to include Dublin Core metadata in your metadata.json file. Without it, your MicroSim won't be discoverable or properly indexed. Every infographic needs a title, creator, and description at minimum.

docs.title: Celebration (Achievements)
docs.text:
Use mascot-celebration at the end of major sections or chapters.

Excellent Progress!

You've just built your first complete interactive infographic with overlay regions, hover-activated infoboxes, and xAPI event logging. That's a fully deployable MicroSim — display it with style!

docs.title: Encourage (Difficult Content)
docs.text:
Use mascot-encourage before or during challenging sections where students often struggle.

You've Got This!

Causal loop diagrams can feel overwhelming at first — all those arrows and polarities! But once you learn to trace one loop at a time and identify whether it's reinforcing or balancing, the system dynamics become clear. Keep going, you're closer than you think!

docs.title: Neutral (General Purpose)
docs.text:
Use mascot-neutral for general sidebars, introductions, or any context where none of the specific poses fits.

A Note from Percy

This course covers many JavaScript visualization libraries — p5.js, D3.js, Chart.js, and vis-network. You don't need to master them all. Focus on one that fits your project, and branch out from there.

docs.title: Usage Rules
docs.text:
• Use Percy no more than 5-6 times per chapter
  • Do not stack multiple Percy admonitions back-to-back
  • Keep Percy's dialogue brief (1-4 sentences)
  • Always use a catchphrase at least once per chapter welcome:
    • "Let's make it visual!"
    • "Display it with style!"
    • "Let's spread some knowledge!"

docs.title: Percy the Peacock - Mascot Test

docs.title: Admonition Tests
docs.text:
Let's Make It Visual!

Welcome, visual thinkers! I'm Percy the Peacock, your guide through the world of interactive infographics. From simple labeled diagrams to complex polygon overlays — let's make it visual!

Key Insight

Notice that every interactive infographic follows the same core pattern: a base image, an overlay configuration, and an event handler. Once you understand this architecture, you can build any type of infographic.

Percy's Tip

Always test your infographics at both 600px and 1400px widths to ensure they're truly responsive. The aliceblue drawing region should scale smoothly without breaking your layout.

Common Mistake

Don't forget to include Dublin Core metadata in your metadata.json file. Without it, your MicroSim won't be discoverable or properly indexed. Every infographic needs a title, creator, and description at minimum.

You've Got This!

Causal loop diagrams can feel overwhelming at first — all those arrows and polarities! But once you learn to trace one loop at a time, the system dynamics become clear. Keep going, you're closer than you think!

Excellent Work!

You've just built your first complete interactive infographic with overlay regions, hover-activated infoboxes, and xAPI event logging. That's a fully deployable MicroSim — display it with style!

A Note from Percy

This course covers many JavaScript visualization libraries — p5.js, D3.js, Chart.js, and vis-network. You don't need to master them all. Focus on one that fits your project, and branch out from there.

docs.title: Do's and Don'ts
docs.text:
Do:

  • Use Percy to introduce new topics warmly
  • Include a catchphrase in welcome admonitions
  • Keep dialogue brief (1-3 sentences)
  • Match the pose/image to the content type

Don't:

  • Use Percy more than 5-6 times per chapter
  • Put mascot admonitions back-to-back
  • Use the mascot for purely decorative purposes
  • Change Percy's personality or speech patterns

docs.title: Cover Image Prompt
docs.text:
Please generate a wide landscape book cover image (1200x630 pixels, 1.91:1 aspect ratio)
for "Interactive Infographics" — a textbook about creating interactive
infographic diagrams for intelligent textbooks.

COMPOSITION: On the LEFT 15% place the image of our course mascot, Percy the Peacock with a welcome pose. The remaining 85% of the image contains the montage elements described below.

TITLE IN CENTER: Place the text "Interactive Infographics" in the center using a white font.

BACKGROUND: Deep royal blue (#1565C0) to dark navy (#0D47A1) gradient
flowing from left to right, with subtle warm orange (#FF7043) accent
glows at key focal points.

MONTAGE ELEMENTS: A rich collage of interactive infographic and data
visualization elements, distributed across the image:
- Colorful bar charts, line graphs, and pie chart segments
- A diagram of cross section of an animal cell
- A small causal loop diagram with reinforcing and balancing loop arrows
- Network graph visualizations with glowing nodes and connecting edges
- Interactive overlay hotspots (small circles with plus icons) on a
  diagram silhouette
- Flowchart arrows and decision diamond shapes
- A timeline bar with milestone markers
- SmartArt-style process arrows and chevron shapes
- A small pyramid/funnel diagram in graduated colors
- Iframe borders suggesting embedded interactive content
- Slider controls, toggle buttons, and other interactive UI widgets at the bottom
- Subtle grid lines and coordinate axes in the background

STYLE: Modern flat vector design aesthetic. Elements rendered as clean,
colorful icons and mini-illustrations with subtle depth shadows. Orange
(#FF7043) and gold (#FFC107) accent highlights on key elements against
the blue background. Elements softly fade and reduce in opacity toward
the edges with a gentle vignette effect.

CENTER AREA: The center-right portion of the image should have a slightly
darker, cleaner area (semi-transparent dark overlay) suitable for white
title text.

TECHNICAL: Professional quality, high resolution, vibrant but cohesive
color palette. No text in the image. No photographs — all elements should
be illustrated/vector style.

docs.title: Compositing Percy the Peacock
docs.text:
After generating the background montage and the Percy welcome pose separately:

  1. Open the cover background image (1200x630)
  2. Place docs/img/mascot/welcome.png on the left side
  3. Position Percy so he occupies roughly the left 10-15% of the image (about 120-180px wide)
  4. Percy should be vertically centered, standing on or near the bottom third
  5. Ensure Percy's transparent background blends naturally with the cover gradient
  6. Export final composite as docs/img/cover.png

Tools for compositing: - macOS Preview (simple overlay) - GIMP (free, full control) - Figma (free tier, easy layering) - Canva (free tier, drag and drop) - ImageMagick command line:

# Example ImageMagick composite command
composite -gravity West -geometry 150x150+20+0 \
  docs/img/mascot/welcome.png \
  docs/img/cover-background.png \
  docs/img/cover.png

docs.title: Adding Title Text (Optional)
docs.text:
If you want to bake the title into the image rather than using HTML/CSS overlay:

  1. Add a semi-transparent dark rectangle (opacity 50%) in the center-right area
  2. Add "Interactive Infographics" in white, bold sans-serif font (Inter, Montserrat, or Source Sans Pro)
  3. Optionally add subtitle: "AI-Powered Visual Learning for Intelligent Textbooks"
  4. Export as PNG at 1200x630 pixels

docs.title: Chapter Content Generation
docs.text:
Prompt

use the /chapter-content-generator on @docs/chapters/01-foundations-of-interactive-infographics/index.md - use a positive optimistic tone about how powerful and critical great interactive infographics can have on a great learning experience.

docs.title: Open Source Tools
docs.text:
Warning

None of these tools will create interactive infographics that conform to the MicroSim standards. They are there only for references.

Prompt

Please assist me by conducting research on the availability of tools for creating consistent, interactive infographics that teachers can use within intelligent textbooks.

Our goal is to develop a streamlined approach to creating detailed, interactive infographics for textbooks that do not require advanced programming skills. We hope to utilize generative AI to make creating infographics more accessible for all teachers. Our infographics need to have clickable or hover regions that we can monitor to gauge whether students are using and learning from them. The current best practice is to use tools like D3.js and p5.js to create infographics. However, these JavaScript libraries are too complex for many teachers with no programming background.

We also need these infographics to follow simple rules to make them consistent with the other infographics on the websites. They should all have a consistent background color and logging of what the students are clicking on

Please analyze the free tools that can make the process of creating consistent interactive infographics easier for teachers. Return a report of these tools and how they can be used to make low-code/no-code infographics. We are interested in mostly free and open-source tools.

docs.title: Initial Response
docs.text:
To ensure I provide the most useful recommendations, could you please clarify the following:

  1. Do you need the tools to support embedding in existing platforms like Moodle, Google Classroom, or custom LMSs?
  2. Is there a specific data format or structure the teachers will be using (e.g., spreadsheets, CSVs, JSON)?
  3. Should the tools support exporting the infographics as standalone HTML/JS files or integrate directly with an AI workflow?
  4. Are there any specific accessibility or device compatibility requirements (e.g., mobile-friendly, screen reader support)?

This will help me tailor the research to fit your goals more closely.

You said:

  1. The tools are not required to work with any existing LMS system like Moodle, although support of xAPI is a bonus.
  2. . There is no specific data format that the teachers will be using; however, standards like JSON are nice
  3. Our hope is that the tools can generate standalone HTML/JS but allow hooks to support user logging and event logs
  4. There are no accessibility or device requirements - any web browser might be used

docs.title: Interactive Infographic Creation Tools for Teachers
docs.text: Creating consistent, interactive infographics for digital textbooks can be made much easier with a variety of free or open-source tools. Many of these solutions allow teachers with little or no programming background to design engaging visuals that include clickable or hoverable regions for student interaction. Below we outline several tools and approaches (primarily low-code or no-code) that can help educators develop interactive infographics, with an emphasis on consistency (e.g. common styles like background colors) and support for logging student interactions (e.g. via xAPI or similar).

docs.title: H5P – Open-Source Interactive Content Creation
docs.text:
Example: A laboratory safety infographic enhanced with interactive hotspots (red “plus” icons) that reveal additional information when clicked. Tools like H5P allow teachers to overlay such clickable hotspots on images without coding, turning static diagrams into interactive learning experiences. This ensures students engage with the visual and can access deeper explanations by clicking on points of interest. In H5P, each hotspot can display text, images, or video in a popup, and authors can configure properties like hotspot color to maintain a consistent look across infographics.

H5P is a popular open-source platform for creating interactive learning content through a web-based editor (no coding required). Originally developed for e-learning, H5P provides many content templates that can serve as interactive infographics or diagrams. Key features and benefits of H5P include:

  1. No-Code Authoring: Teachers create content using a user-friendly editor in a web browser or via the Lumi desktop app (which allows offline H5P content editing). No advanced programming is needed – interactive elements are added through form inputs and uploads.

  2. Image Hotspots and Other Content Types: H5P’s Image Hotspots content type lets you upload an image (e.g. a diagram or map) and place clickable hotspots on it. When students click these hotspots, a pop-up can show explanatory text, images, or even videos. This is ideal for turning a static infographic into an interactive one with additional information on demand. For example, a teacher can create an infographic and use hotspots to explain different parts of a diagram or highlight data points. (H5P also offers other interactive content types that might be useful, such as interactive timelines, quizzes, charts, etc.)

  3. Consistency in Design: H5P content types come with a modern, clean design out of the box. Authors can ensure consistency by using the same content type and configuring the same color scheme or icon set for all infographics. For instance, the Image Hotspots tool allows choosing hotspot icon shapes and colors, so all infographics on a site can use a matching style. Additional custom styling (via CSS) is possible if you host H5P on your own website or LMS, enabling consistent background colors or fonts across all infographics.

  4. Embedding and Standalone Use: H5P content can be embedded in any webpage or integrated into learning platforms. You can host H5P on platforms like WordPress, Moodle, or Drupal via plugins, or use the H5P.com service for hosting. In all cases, the result is standalone HTML5 content (JavaScript + HTML) that can be viewed in a browser, which fits the requirement of generating standalone HTML/JS for an intelligent textbook.

5 Logging and xAPI Support: H5P content is built with an xAPI (Experience API) under the hood. That means as students interact (e.g. answer a quiz, finish watching a video, or even click on certain interactive elements), H5P can emit xAPI statements that record these events. When H5P is integrated into an LMS like Moodle or other platforms, instructors can track student engagement and responses. For example, if a hotspot activity is structured as a quiz or exploration, you could capture which hotspots the student clicked. (In practice, some H5P content types have more detailed xAPI tracking than others – quiz-type activities report scores, while simple hotspot clicks might require custom handling – but the infrastructure for capturing interactions is there.) Additionally, third-party plugins (e.g. H5P xAPI connectors or LRS integrations) exist to collect detailed interaction logs for H5P content. This makes it feasible to monitor whether students are engaging with the infographic and what they click on, satisfying the requirement for usage tracking.

Overall, H5P provides an easy entry point for educators to create rich interactive infographics. It is free and open-source, and it has an active community sharing content types. By using H5P, a teacher can maintain consistency (using the same templates and style choices for all infographics) and integrate student interaction logging with minimal setup.

docs.title: Xerte – Flexible Toolkit for Interactive Learning
docs.text:
Like H5P, Xerte is an open-source authoring tool that enables teachers to create interactive learning materials without needing programming skills. Xerte (often referred to as Xerte Online Toolkits) is a browser-based suite that allows assembling content pages with text, media, and interactive components. It’s a bit more of an all-in-one authoring environment for building interactive lessons or modules, but it includes features that work well for infographics:

  • No-Code, Template-Based Authoring: Xerte provides a range of page templates for different interactive activities. For example, there is an “Annotated Diagram” page type specifically designed for images with clickable regions. Using this template, a teacher can upload an image (diagram, infographic, etc.) and define multiple hotspots or zones on the image. Each hotspot can be associated with a label and descriptive text that appears when the student clicks that region. This is very similar in outcome to H5P’s image hotspots: the learner clicks on parts of the image to reveal additional information.
  • Consistent Styling and Layout: Xerte emphasizes consistency by using a template approach. All pages of a given type have a uniform look, and global design settings can be applied. For instance, authors can choose a background color or theme for their Xerte project, ensuring all pages (and thus all infographics within that project) share the same background and font style. Even within an interactive image, Xerte allows configuring the highlight style (e.g. choosing the highlight color or shape for the hotspot indicator) – the documentation suggests making a list of preferred color codes to reuse for consistency across projects. This way, a set of infographics created in Xerte can all adhere to a common visual style.
  • Accessibility and Device Compatibility: Xerte has strong built-in support for accessibility (e.g. high-contrast mode, alt text for images, keyboard navigation). This means infographics created with Xerte’s templates can be more accessible to all learners by default. The content is web-based HTML5, so it will work on modern web browsers without additional plugins.
  • Tracking Student Interactions: Xerte projects can be deployed in various ways (as a standalone web link or embedded object). It supports integration standards like SCORM for LMS tracking, and newer versions have options for xAPI (via an LRS) in some cases. According to instructional designers’ experiences, Xerte allows authors to track learner engagement and even build in quiz questions or decision paths to monitor progress. For a simple interactive infographic (like an annotated image), Xerte could record which hotspots were clicked if set up as a graded interaction. In scenarios where it’s not a formal quiz, teachers could still infer engagement (e.g. Xerte might report that the page was viewed or completed). Each Xerte project also generates a unique URL, making it easy to share and possibly wrap in an LMS or an LRS for capturing analytics.
  • Advanced Interactivity and Logic: Xerte is quite powerful in that beyond simple hotspots, it supports branching scenarios, quizzes, and even gamified elements (timers, scores) if needed. This might be beyond the basic infographic, but it means a teacher could create an interactive infographic that includes quiz questions or an exploratory path. For instance, an infographic could have hotspot questions that ask students to identify certain parts – Xerte’s flexibility would allow building that with no coding. This tool might have a slightly higher learning curve than H5P for some, but it also offers more customization if needed (including the ability to add custom HTML or scripts for further tailoring, though that requires some tech skill).

In summary, Xerte is another excellent free, open-source option for low-code interactive content creation. It’s well-suited for building consistent, self-contained interactive textbook elements. Teachers can use its templates (like the annotated image hotspot) to quickly turn images into interactive infographics, with the added benefits of accessibility features and potential tracking of student interactions. Xerte and H5P have overlapping capabilities, and in practice many educators use them complementarily – H5P for quick embeds and Xerte for more elaborate interactive lessons. For the specific use-case of interactive infographics, both are viable: H5P might be a bit simpler to get started, whereas Xerte offers deeper customization and control over the learning experience.

docs.title: Charticulator – Custom Charts and Infographics Without Coding
docs.text:
While H5P and Xerte focus on broader e-learning content, Charticulator is a specialized tool for designing custom data visualizations (charts) in a no-code manner. Developed by Microsoft Research, Charticulator is free and open-source software that runs in the browser. Its primary use is to let users create bespoke charts or graphical representations from data without writing D3 or JavaScript code.

  • Drag-and-Drop Chart Design: In Charticulator, you load a dataset (e.g. from a spreadsheet or CSV) and then visually drag and position graphical elements to represent the data. It differs from conventional chart tools by not limiting you to predefined chart types. You can design unique infographic-style charts (like custom maps, pictograms, multi-layered visuals) using its graphical interface. This is useful for teachers who want a specific visual to illustrate a concept but don’t have the coding expertise to craft it in D3/p5. For example, a teacher could create an interactive bar chart where each bar is an icon, or a custom timeline with unique markers – all without coding, by using Charticulator’s constraints-based design system.
  • Interactive Outputs: Charticulator allows exporting the created visualization as standalone HTML/JavaScript (or as an SVG image). The HTML export uses D3.js under the hood to render the chart, meaning the result can include interactive behaviors like tooltips, hover highlights, or basic click interactions. For instance, if you design a chart with Charticulator, by default it might support hovering to show data values or clicking legend items to filter – this depends on how it’s set up. While it’s not as full-featured as a hand-coded D3 graphic, it does produce a self-contained interactive infographic piece that can be embedded in a webpage or intelligent textbook. Teachers can therefore create a data-driven infographic and drop the HTML/JS output into their site.
  • Reusable Templates for Consistency: Charticulator designs can be saved and reused. If an educator or design team creates a particular style (color scheme, font, layout) for charts, they can replicate that style across multiple charts by reusing the Charticulator template or starting from a previous design. This helps ensure all infographics in the textbook have a consistent visual identity. Since you have fine control over design in Charticulator, you can match the background color, typography, and other style elements to whatever theme your textbook uses.
  • No Programming Skill Needed: The major advantage is democratizing what traditionally required coding. Charticulator was explicitly created to “enable the creation of bespoke interactive chart designs without the need for coding”. It sits between template-driven chart tools and full programming, giving a lot of flexibility through a visual interface. Teachers can achieve near D3-level custom visuals with far less effort. That said, mastering Charticulator does require learning its interface and concepts (like “glyphs” and “constraints”), but there are tutorials and a gallery of examples to draw inspiration from.
  • Integration and Logging: By itself, Charticulator does not provide learning analytics or xAPI logging – it’s primarily a visualization designer. If you embed a Charticulator-produced chart into a page, tracking clicks or hovers would require adding custom JavaScript listeners to the embedded chart code. This is possible (since the output is HTML/JS, a developer could instrument it), but it isn’t a built-in feature. Charticulator is best used in combination with other frameworks: for example, you might embed a Charticulator chart inside an H5P “Interactive Book” or within a Xerte page. The surrounding framework (H5P, Xerte, or your website code) could then handle capturing events. If detailed student interaction logging on the chart is crucial (e.g. which parts of the chart they clicked), some custom coding would be needed. However, if the main goal is an interactive visualization (with tooltips, etc.) that looks consistent and is easy for a teacher to create, Charticulator is a powerful option.

In essence, Charticulator expands the range of infographics educators can create by enabling custom data visualizations without coding. It complements the likes of H5P and Xerte: those tools might cover interactive text/image content, while Charticulator covers data-heavy infographics (charts, graphs) in a teacher-friendly way. Using Charticulator, one can maintain stylistic consistency and embed the resulting charts into broader interactive content. The learning curve is a bit higher than point-and-click quiz tools, but it remains far easier than coding from scratch in D3 or p5.

docs.title: Generative AI for Infographic Design (Emerging Tools)
docs.text:
Because the question specifically mentions leveraging generative AI to simplify infographic creation, it’s worth noting some recent developments in this space. Generative AI can assist teachers by automating parts of the design or coding process for interactive infographics:

  • AI-Powered Infographic Generators: Some commercial tools have introduced AI features that generate infographics from a prompt or dataset. For example, Infogram (a popular online infographic and chart maker) has an AI Infographic Maker that can suggest layouts and create visuals based on a short description or data input. This AI helps users with little design experience quickly produce a professional-looking infographic, which can then be tweaked manually. Similarly, other services like Piktochart have announced AI features to create draft infographics from text. These platforms often support interactivity (Infogram, for instance, allows charts and maps that are interactive, and you can embed links or hover tooltips in the infographic). However, such services are typically freemium (not fully open-source), and the AI features are relatively new. They are worth watching as they evolve, because they point toward a future where a teacher could simply describe the infographic they need (“an interactive timeline of World War II events” or “a clickable diagram of the water cycle”) and let AI draft it.
  • Using LLMs to Generate Code or JSON: Another way generative AI can help is by producing the underlying code or configuration for interactive visuals. For tech-savvy teachers, large language models (like ChatGPT) can be prompted to generate D3.js or p5.js code for a given visualization. For instance, a teacher might ask an AI to “create an HTML/JS code for an interactive bar chart with tooltips showing these data points…”. The model can output a starter code which the teacher (or a developer collaborator) could then refine. This still requires some coding ability to implement, but it lowers the barrier by providing a template that the teacher didn’t have to write from scratch. In the context of our low-code tools: one could imagine an AI that generates a configuration file for H5P or Xerte. While not available yet, it’s conceivable to have a system where a teacher says “I have an image of the human heart, label the chambers with hotspots that give definitions,” and an AI could configure an H5P Image Hotspot content automatically (filling in the texts). In short, AI can act as a smart assistant in setting up the infographic.
  • AI for Image Generation and Enhancement: Generative AI (like DALL-E or Midjourney) can also create illustrations or icons to use in your infographic. While this is slightly aside from interactive functionality, a teacher could quickly generate custom graphics (e.g. an original illustration of a historical scene or a scientific diagram) using AI image generators, and then use the above tools (H5P, Xerte, etc.) to make them interactive. This reduces the need for artistic skills or finding stock images, making the whole creation process more accessible.
  • Considerations: When using AI-based tools, keep in mind issues of accuracy and editorial control. AI might accelerate the mechanical steps of creation, but a human expert (the teacher) should review and adjust the content to ensure it meets learning objectives and is factually correct. Also, not all AI-generated solutions will seamlessly integrate with tracking – a generated infographic might still need to be placed inside a platform that logs clicks (or the AI tool itself might offer analytics, as Infogram does with its engagement analytics feature).

Currently, the most teacher-friendly approach is to use the likes of H5P or Xerte for the interactivity and consider AI as a helper to reduce design effort. For example, a workflow might be: use an AI tool to draft a base infographic or gather data, then import that into H5P to add interactive hotspots and ensure the piece follows your site’s style and tracking requirements.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text:
In summary, there are several free or open-source tools available to help educators create interactive infographics with minimal coding:

  • H5P and Xerte stand out as educator-oriented tools that provide templated interactivity (like image hotspots, timelines, quizzes) via graphical interfaces. They enable consistency in design and offer pathways for tracking student interactions (through LMS integration or xAPI). These would allow teachers to enrich textbook visuals with interactive pop-ups, all while keeping a uniform style across the course.
  • Charticulator addresses the need for custom charts and data visuals without coding, letting teachers design unique infographics and export them as standalone interactive graphics. It requires a bit more design effort but opens up possibilities for data-driven content that matches the textbook’s look.
  • Generative AI is an emerging aid – it can streamline the creation process by producing design suggestions or even code, though it’s largely available in commercial tools at present. As AI integration improves, we can expect even more accessible “natural language to infographic” workflows, which could further lower the barrier for teachers.

Using these tools, a teacher can create interactive infographic content that has a consistent appearance (common backgrounds, colors, fonts) and includes the necessary hooks to monitor student engagement (clicks, hovers, quiz answers, etc.). Best of all, the solutions listed prioritize ease of use and are either free or open-source, making them practical for wide adoption in educational settings. By combining such tools with thoughtful design (and perhaps a touch of AI assistance), intelligent textbooks can offer rich, interactive infographics that enhance learning without demanding advanced programming knowledge from every teacher.

Sources:

  • Mendi Benigni, “Using Lumi and H5P to create Easy Images with Hotspots…” – TLT Blog (2022)
  • H5P Documentation – “Image Hotspots” (open-source interactive content type)
  • University of Bath TEL Team – “Exploring Interactive Learning: Xerte and H5P…” (2025)
  • Xerte Community Wiki – “Annotated Diagram page type” (2025)
  • Ajit Jaokar, DataScienceCentral – “Charticulator: Creating Interactive Charts Without Code” (2021)
  • Infogram – “Create Interactive Infographics with AI Generator” (feature page, 2023)
  • H5P Developer Docs – Forum/QA on xAPI and tracking user interactions (demonstrating H5P emits xAPI statements for actions)

docs.title: List of Interactive Infographic MicroSims
docs.text:
• Animal Cell

    Overlay-style MicroSim that spotlights six core organelles with hover callouts, AP tips, and a quiz mode for quick recall checks. Use it as a template for single-diagram explorations.

  • Biogeochemical Cycles Dashboard

    Tabbed dashboard that overlays the carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, and water cycles on one cross-sectional landscape. Toggle reservoirs, human impacts, and narrated overlays to trace atoms through shared ecosystems.

  • Brewing Beer Process Explorer

    Five-stage walkthrough of alcoholic fermentation that pairs brewery equipment cutaways with molecular-scale reactions. Students can jump between stages, reveal annotations, and read the biochemical story as glucose becomes ethanol.

  • Comparative Anatomy Explorer

    Forelimbs from five vertebrates appear side by side; hovering over a bone highlights the homologous structure in every species. Quiz mode asks learners to classify homologous, analogous, or vestigial pairs with instant feedback.

  • Prokaryote vs. Eukaryote Comparison

    Dual-panel diagram contrasting bacterial and animal cells with 15 labeled structures, AP exam reminders, and a quiz pathway for structure identification. Edit mode lets authors fine-tune marker coordinates.

  • Learning Graph Viewer

    Interactive force-directed viewer that loads the book's learning graph so you can search, filter by category, pan/zoom the network, and view live statistics on visible nodes and edges.

docs.title: Animal Cell MicroSim
docs.text: View the Animal Cell MicroSim Fullscreen

docs.title: Lesson Plan: Exploring the Animal Cell
docs.text: Audience: AP Biology or advanced high-school biology students Duration: 45–60 minutes Materials: Animal Cell MicroSim (embedded above), student notebooks or digital lab journal, projector (optional)

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this lesson, students will be able to:

  1. Identify the nucleus, cell membrane, cytoplasm, ribosomes, mitochondria, and endoplasmic reticulum on a schematic animal cell.
  2. Explain how structure supports function for each organelle, citing at least one specific detail (e.g., cristae of mitochondria, pores of the nuclear envelope).
  3. Trace the movement of a newly synthesized protein through the endomembrane system using the MicroSim annotations.
  4. Connect each organelle to a relevant AP Biology concept or exam tip (e.g., selective permeability, endosymbiotic theory, glycolysis location).

docs.title: 1. Engage (5 minutes)
docs.text: • Project the MicroSim and hide callout labels (click the “i” icons off) to display the cell diagram without text. Ask: “If a virus wanted to take over this cell, which structure would it target first and why?” Discuss initial guesses to surface prior knowledge.

docs.title: 2. Explore (15 minutes)
docs.text:
Students work in pairs with laptops:

  • Click each hotspot to reveal the organelle description and AP tip.
  • Record two facts per organelle in a T-chart: Structure Detail vs. Function/Process Supported.
  • Prompt questions:
  • Nucleus: How do nuclear pores support rapid transcription-response cycles?
  • Cell membrane: Which molecules move freely vs. require a transport protein?
  • Mitochondria: Where exactly do the Krebs cycle and electron transport chain occur?
  • Cytoplasm: Which reactions stay here rather than moving into organelles?
  • Ribosomes: How do free vs. bound ribosomes decide a protein’s destination?
  • Endoplasmic Reticulum: What differentiates rough and smooth ER roles?

docs.title: 3. Explain (15 minutes)
docs.text:
Facilitate a class discussion anchored in the MicroSim:

  • Mini whiteboard share-out: Each pair chooses one organelle and sketches it quickly, labeling one key structural feature from the sim (e.g., folded cristae).
  • Protein journey walkthrough: Use the callouts to narrate the path of a hormone protein: transcription in the nucleus → ribosome on rough ER → ER lumen processing → transport vesicle → Golgi (describe even if not shown) → plasma membrane secretion. Emphasize the AP exam “endomembrane system” terminology noted in the ER callout.
  • Highlight AP tips embedded in the data (e.g., endosymbiotic evidence in mitochondria, glycolysis location in cytoplasm) and connect them to past FRQs or MCQ distractors.

docs.title: 4. Elaborate (10 minutes)
docs.text:
Assign quick synthesis tasks:

  • Option A: Students write a 4–5 sentence “cell diary” entry from the perspective of an organelle, referencing at least one fact from the MicroSim callout.
  • Option B: Challenge learners to design a mutation or toxin that disrupts one structure shown, predicting downstream impacts on the cell.

docs.title: 5. Evaluate (Exit Ticket)
docs.text:
• Prompt: “Match each process to its correct location: glycolysis, electron transport chain, mRNA translation for secreted proteins, lipid detoxification, selective permeability.” Students submit a short response citing at least three organelles.
  • Alternatively, use a quick auto-graded form with image hotspots derived from the sim.

docs.title: Differentiation & Extensions
docs.text:
• Support: Provide a printed diagram with labels for students who need scaffolding; allow them to annotate directly while others use the interactive version.
  • Extension: Have advanced students compare the animal cell MicroSim to a plant cell diagram, listing which structures are unique and hypothesizing how the MicroSim could be expanded.
  • Cross-topic tie-ins: Link mitochondria to the cellular respiration chapter, ribosomes to molecular genetics, and cell membrane concepts to membrane transport simulations elsewhere in the course.

docs.title: Assessment Ideas
docs.text:
• Short quiz asking students to drag labels onto the correct organelles (screenshots from the sim).
  • FRQ practice: “Explain how defects in the rough ER or mitochondria would differentially impact ATP production and protein secretion.”
  • Peer teaching: students create a brief screencast walking through three organelles using the MicroSim, graded with a rubric focusing on accuracy and explanatory clarity.

docs.title: Teacher Notes
docs.text:
• The MicroSim callouts include AP-specific reminders (e.g., ribosome size differences, endosymbiotic theory). Encourage students to capture these in their notebooks as “exam traps to avoid.”
  • If bandwidth is limited, download the main.html bundle locally so each group can run the simulation offline.
  • Reinforce vocabulary precision: nucleus vs. nucleolus, cytoplasm vs. cytosol, rough vs. smooth ER. These distinctions often appear in AP free-response questions.

docs.title: Biogeochemical Cycles Dashboard
docs.text: Run the Biogeochemical Cycles Dashboard MicroSim Fullscreen Edit the Biogeochemical Cycles Dashboard MicroSim Using the p5.js Editor

docs.title: About This MicroSim
docs.text: This MicroSim turns a richly layered landscape illustration into a dashboard for tracking how matter flows through the carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, and water cycles. The static art shows mountains, forests, farmland, wetlands, ocean waters, and underground strata so learners can visually anchor each reservoir. Interactive tabs fade in overlays for each cycle, highlighting reservoirs, process arrows, and human impacts such as combustion or fertilizer runoff. The goal is to help students compare how the same landscape supports multiple biogeochemical loops.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Move through the tabs to focus on one cycle at a time; the legend explains what each color-coded glow or arrow means.
  2. Hover markers to read short explanations of reservoirs, fluxes, and human interventions tied to the artwork (e.g., the smokestack plume or aquifer).
  3. Toggle the “Human Impact” layer to compare the balanced natural cycle with altered flows such as eutrophication, fossil fuel combustion, or deforestation.
  4. Challenge yourself to trace a specific atom (carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, or water) from source to sink across at least three reservoirs.

docs.title: Iframe Embed Code
docs.text:
You can add this MicroSim to any web page by adding this to your HTML:

docs.title: Grade Level
docs.text: 9-12 (AP/IB Biology or Earth Science)

docs.title: Duration
docs.text: 10-15 minutes

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Understanding that matter cycles through reservoirs instead of being newly created or destroyed.
  • Familiarity with reading diagrams and legends.
  • Prior exposure to an individual cycle (carbon or water) so students can scale up to multiple cycles at once.

docs.title: Activities
docs.text:
1. Exploration (5 min): Free navigation through all tabs while students jot down observed reservoirs and fluxes unique to each cycle.
  2. Guided Practice (5 min): Assign a “follow the atom” prompt (e.g., trace a nitrogen atom from lightning to the ocean floor) and have students verbalize each path using the overlays.
  3. Assessment (5 min): Provide a short worksheet asking students to identify the two reservoirs most affected by human activity in each cycle and justify their choice referencing the dashboard.

docs.title: Assessment
docs.text:
• Diagram annotations or written responses describing at least three reservoirs, two fluxes, and one human impact per cycle.
  • Exit ticket question: “Which reservoir serves as the long-term sink for each cycle, and how do you know?” Students must reference visual evidence.
  • Optional extension: Students design a mitigation strategy (e.g., riparian buffer) and describe how it would change the overlays.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration. “Carbon Cycle.” Accessed
  2. https://sos.noaa.gov/datasets/carbon-cycle/
  3. NASA Earth Observatory. “Water Cycle.” Accessed 2024. https://earthobservatory.nasa.gov/features/Water
  4. U.S. Geological Survey. “Nutrient Cycling in Agricultural Landscapes.” Accessed 2024. https://www.usgs.gov/mission-areas/water-resources

docs.title: How to Use Five Levels of Intelligent Textbooks Infographic in Your Classroom
docs.text: This guide explains how to use the interactive "Five Levels of Intelligent Textbooks" MicroSim to understand the progression from traditional static textbooks to advanced AI-driven educational resources. The iframe above makes it easy to include on any website.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The MicroSim presents a visual stair-step diagram showing the five levels of intelligent textbooks:

  1. Level 1: Static Textbooks (Red)
  2. Level 2: Interactive Content Textbooks (Blue)
  3. Level 3: Adaptive Textbooks (Teal)
  4. Level 4: Textbooks with Chatbots (Purple)
  5. Level 5: Autonomous AI Textbooks (Gold)

docs.title: How to Interact with the MicroSim
docs.text:
1. Hover Interaction: Move your cursor over any of the five colored step levels to display detailed information about that level.
  2. Touch Interaction: On touch devices, tap a step to see its description.
  3. Responsive Design: The visualization automatically adjusts to your screen size, making it accessible on various devices.

docs.title: Level 1: Static Textbooks
docs.text:
• Characteristics: Traditional printed or digital formats with no interactive elements
  • Usage: Over 90% of college textbooks remain at this level
  • Applications: Suitable for simple content delivery where interaction isn't necessary

docs.title: Level 2: Interactive Content Textbooks
docs.text:
• Characteristics: Digital elements that engage readers beyond passive consumption
  • Features: Keyword search, hyperlinks, embedded videos, simple quizzes, AI-generated MicroSims
  • Benefits: Cost-effective enhancements that improve engagement with multimedia elements

docs.title: Level 3: Adaptive Textbooks
docs.text:
• Characteristics: Dynamic content adjustment based on user input and performance
  • Features: Personalized learning pathways, concept graph traversal, performance-based content selection
  • Implementation: Requires data management systems and graph algorithms
  • Privacy Caution: These systems collect and analyze student learning data to provide adaptivity, raising important privacy considerations that educators should address when implementing

docs.title: Level 4: Textbooks with Chatbots
docs.text:
• Characteristics: Integration of intelligent conversational interfaces
  • Features: LLM-powered tutoring assistants, GraphRAG architecture combining multiple AI technologies
  • Implementation: Balances powerful LLMs with cost-effective smaller models
  • Privacy Caution: Interactions with chatbots involve collecting potentially sensitive student questions and responses; institutions should implement proper data protection measures and transparency about how this interaction data is used

docs.title: Level 5: Autonomous AI Textbooks
docs.text:
• Characteristics: Future systems that fully understand individual learner needs
  • Features: Deep understanding of student knowledge, real-time generation of customized lessons
  • Current Status: Aspirational, requiring advanced hardware and more reliable LLMs
  • Privacy Caution: The most advanced system would require extensive student data collection, including detailed cognitive and behavioral patterns; the educational benefits must be balanced against stringent privacy protections and ethical considerations about AI autonomy in educational settings

docs.title: Educational Applications
docs.text:
• Comparative Analysis: Use the MicroSim to compare the features and capabilities of different textbook technologies
  • Educational Planning: Help administrators understand the progression of educational technology to make informed decisions about textbook adoption
  • Student Information: Introduce students to the different types of learning resources they might encounter in their educational journey

docs.title: Privacy Considerations Across Levels
docs.text:
As textbooks advance from static (Level 1) to autonomous (Level 5), data collection and privacy concerns increase significantly:

  • Level 1-2: Minimal privacy concerns as little or no student-specific data is collected
  • Level 3: Begins collecting student performance and behavior data to enable adaptation
  • Level 4: Stores conversation histories and student queries that may contain personal information
  • Level 5: Would require comprehensive student profiling to deliver fully personalized experiences

Educational institutions implementing higher-level intelligent textbooks should:

  1. Develop clear data privacy policies
  2. Obtain informed consent from students
  3. Implement robust data security measures
  4. Consider data minimization principles
  5. Provide transparency about how AI systems use student data
  6. Offer opt-out options where feasible

docs.title: Technical Notes
docs.text:
The MicroSim is built using p5.js and adapts to different screen sizes by: - Adjusting step sizes and text formatting based on screen width - Shortening labels on smaller screens for better readability - Maintaining touch functionality for mobile devices

By exploring this MicroSim, users can gain a deeper understanding of how educational content is evolving with technology and the important considerations that come with these advancements.

docs.title: Brewing Beer Process Explorer
docs.text: Run the Brewing Beer Process Explorer MicroSim Fullscreen

docs.title: About This MicroSim
docs.text:
This MicroSim traces the complete biochemical pathway of alcoholic fermentation as it occurs inside a brewery fermenter. Students step through five illustrated stages that follow a glucose molecule from crushed grain to ethanol in the finished beer. Each stage pairs a detailed cutaway illustration of brewing equipment with a magnified inset showing the molecular-scale reactions inside a yeast cell.

The five stages map directly to the AP Biology cellular respiration and fermentation curriculum:

  1. Glucose Feedstock Preparation — starch hydrolysis by amylase enzymes
  2. Glycolysis — the ten-enzyme cascade producing pyruvate, ATP, and NADH
  3. Anaerobic Pyruvate Accumulation — the metabolic bottleneck when oxygen is excluded
  4. Decarboxylation — pyruvate decarboxylase releasing $\ce{CO2}$ and acetaldehyde
  5. Ethanol Formation — alcohol dehydrogenase regenerating $\ce{NAD+}$ to keep glycolysis running

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Click any Step button (1 through 5) to jump to that stage of the process.
  2. Toggle Show Labels to overlay or hide the labeled callouts on each image. Labels identify both the brewing equipment (mash tun, fermenter, blow-off hose) and the molecular components (glucose, pyruvate, NADH, enzymes).
  3. Read the infobox below the controls for a detailed explanation of the biochemistry happening at each stage, including the key molecules involved.
  4. Step through the stages in order (1 through 5) to see the full narrative arc of how sugar becomes alcohol and carbon dioxide.

docs.title: Glycolysis (Step 2)
docs.text:
Glycolysis is the universal first stage of glucose metabolism. It occurs in the cytoplasm and does not require oxygen. Each glucose molecule is split into two pyruvate molecules through a ten-enzyme cascade, netting:

  • 2 ATP (substrate-level phosphorylation)
  • 2 NADH (electron carriers)

docs.title: The Anaerobic Bottleneck (Step 3)
docs.text:
When the brewer seals the fermenter, dissolved oxygen is quickly consumed. Without oxygen, the electron transport chain in the mitochondria shuts down. This means:

  • Pyruvate cannot enter the mitochondria for the citric acid cycle
  • NADH cannot donate electrons to the ETC
  • $\ce{NAD+}$ becomes scarce, threatening to stall glycolysis

Fermentation (Steps 4 and 5) solves this bottleneck.

docs.title: Alcoholic Fermentation (Steps 4 and 5)
docs.text:
Fermentation is a two-reaction pathway that regenerates $\ce{NAD+}$:

Reaction 1 — Decarboxylation (Step 4):

$$\ce{pyruvate ->[\text{pyruvate decarboxylase}] acetaldehyde + CO2}$$

This reaction produces all the $\ce{CO2}$ in beer — the bubbles, the foam head, and the carbonation.

Reaction 2 — Reduction (Step 5):

$$\ce{acetaldehyde + NADH + H+ ->[\text{alcohol dehydrogenase}] ethanol + NAD+}$$

The regenerated $\ce{NAD+}$ cycles back to glycolysis, allowing ATP production to continue indefinitely under anaerobic conditions.

docs.title: Net Equation
docs.text:
$$\ce{C6H12O6 -> 2 C2H5OH + 2 CO2 + 2 ATP}$$

Fermentation yields only 2 ATP per glucose — far less than the 30-38 ATP from aerobic respiration — but it allows the yeast to survive and reproduce when oxygen is unavailable.

docs.title: AP Biology Connections
docs.text:
AP Big Idea Connection Big Idea 1: Evolution Fermentation is an ancient metabolic pathway conserved across yeasts, bacteria, and animal muscle cells Big Idea 2: Energetics Glycolysis + fermentation illustrate substrate-level phosphorylation and the necessity of $\ce{NAD+}$ recycling Big Idea 3: Information Enzyme specificity (pyruvate decarboxylase vs. lactate dehydrogenase) determines whether ethanol or lactate is produced Big Idea 4: Systems The sealed fermenter is a model closed system for studying anaerobic metabolism

Common AP Exam Misconception: Students often believe fermentation produces ATP. In fact, glycolysis produces the ATP. Fermentation's sole purpose is to regenerate $\ce{NAD+}$ so that glycolysis can continue.

docs.title: Grade Level
docs.text: 9-12 (High School Biology / AP Biology)

docs.title: Duration
docs.text: 45 minutes (one class period)

docs.title: AP Biology Standards Alignment
docs.text:
• Essential Knowledge 3.D.1 — Cell communication and responses to internal changes
  • Learning Objective ENE-1.L — Explain how cellular respiration and fermentation generate ATP
  • Science Practice 1.4 — Use representations to describe biological concepts

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Students should be familiar with:

  • Basic cell structure (cytoplasm, mitochondria, cell membrane)
  • The concept of enzymes as biological catalysts
  • ATP as the cell's energy currency
  • The difference between aerobic and anaerobic conditions

docs.title: Materials
docs.text:
• Computer or tablet with internet access
  • Student worksheet (see Assessment section)
  • Optional: actual brewing yeast sample and sugar water for a live fermentation demo

docs.title: Warm-Up (5 minutes)
docs.text:
Ask students: "What do bread rising and beer brewing have in common?"

Collect responses on the board. Guide discussion toward the idea that both involve yeast consuming sugar and producing gas ($\ce{CO2}$). Introduce the question that will drive the lesson: "What exactly happens inside a yeast cell when it turns sugar into alcohol and carbon dioxide?"

docs.title: Direct Instruction — Walking Through the Diagram (10 minutes)
docs.text: Project the MicroSim fullscreen with labels ON. Walk through each step as a class, pointing out the specific visual elements in the diagram. The illustrations use a consistent color code throughout all five steps — help students learn to read the molecular shapes so they can follow the biochemical story visually.

docs.title: Step 1 — Glucose Feedstock Preparation
docs.text:
Show the diagram. On the left, point out the copper mash tun filled with crushed barley grains splitting open. Trace the stainless piping to the conical fermenter on the right. Direct students to the magnified inset circle in the lower right — this is where the molecular action happens.

Inside the inset, identify:

  • Starch chains — long, coiled tan spirals made of linked hexagonal units. These are the stored energy in barley grain.
  • Amylase enzymes — blue-green wedge shapes that cleave the starch chains at specific points, like molecular scissors.
  • Maltose — two linked gold hexagons, the primary sugar released by amylase.
  • Glucose monomers — single gold hexagons with a warm glow. This is the fuel that yeast will consume. Every subsequent reaction starts with this molecule.

Guiding question: "Why does the brewer need to crush the grain and add hot water before the yeast can do anything?"

docs.title: Step 2 — Glycolysis in the Fermenter
docs.text:
Click Step 2. The scene shifts to the interior of the sealed fermenter filled with amber wort. Tiny $\ce{CO2}$ bubbles are beginning to form along the vessel walls. Dozens of oval yeast cells (tan cell walls, pale cream cytoplasm) float suspended in the liquid. Note the instrumentation probes (temperature, pH, dissolved oxygen) inserted through the fermenter wall.

Direct students to the inset circle to follow glycolysis:

  • Glucose (warm-gold hexagon) enters the cell from the left through a transporter channel in the cell membrane.
  • Enzyme chain — a row of ten small, distinctly colored shapes arranged left to right, like stations on an assembly line. Each enzyme reshapes the molecule slightly as it passes through.
  • Pyruvate — two orange-red triangular molecules emerge at the right end of the chain. These are the 3-carbon products of splitting the 6-carbon glucose.
  • ATP — bright yellow glowing spheres floating above the enzyme chain (2 per glucose). This is the energy payoff.
  • NADH — violet glowing spheres floating above the chain (2 per glucose). These electron carriers will become critically important in Steps 3 and 5.
  • Mitochondria — faint oval organelles with internal folds (cristae) pushed to the edge of the cell. They are present but not yet relevant — glycolysis happens in the cytoplasm, not the mitochondria.

Guiding question: "What are the two energy-carrying products of glycolysis, and what colors represent them in the diagram?" (Answer: yellow ATP, violet NADH)

docs.title: Step 3 — Pyruvate Pool Under Anaerobic Conditions
docs.text:
Click Step 3. The overall scene has shifted dramatically — the fermenter is now sealed with a tri-clamp lid, and the lighting has changed to cool blue-gray tones emphasizing the anaerobic environment. A blow-off hose runs from the top of the fermenter into a sanitizer bucket on the floor. A faint $\ce{CO2}$ headspace haze sits above the wort surface.

In the inset, the mood has changed inside the cell:

  • Pyruvate molecules (orange-red triangles) are now crowding the cytoplasm — they have nowhere to go because the mitochondria are blocked.
  • NADH (violet glowing spheres) are accumulating in clusters — they cannot donate their electrons to the electron transport chain.
  • Blocked mitochondria — the two mitochondria along the right side of the cell now have their membrane channels visibly closed with small red barrier marks. The internal cristae are dim and inactive.
  • NAD+ (gray spheres, no glow) are scattered sparsely — they are running out because NADH is not being recycled.

This is the metabolic crisis: without $\ce{NAD+}$, glycolysis cannot continue, and ATP production stops. Ask students to notice the contrast between the abundant violet NADH and the scarce gray $\ce{NAD+}$.

Guiding question: "What would happen to the cell if it could not find a way to convert the violet NADH back into gray NAD+?"

docs.title: Step 4 — Decarboxylation: Acetaldehyde and $\ce{CO2}$ Release
docs.text:
Click Step 4. The fermenter is now in peak fermentation — vigorous $\ce{CO2}$ bubbles stream upward, a thick krausen foam crown sits at the wort surface, and the blow-off hose is actively bubbling. The lighting has shifted back to warm amber-orange, reflecting the intense metabolic activity. Outside the fermenter, a brewer's hydrometer floats in a sample jar.

In the inset, point out the first fermentation enzyme:

  • Pyruvate decarboxylase — a large, multi-lobed blue-purple protein complex dominating the center of the cell. It has a visible cleft (the active site) where the reaction occurs.
  • TPP cofactor — a small green diamond shape nestled inside the enzyme's active site. TPP (thiamine pyrophosphate, derived from vitamin B1) is essential for this reaction to work.
  • Pyruvate (orange-red triangle) enters the enzyme active site.
  • $\ce{CO2}$ — small translucent white spheres being ejected upward from the enzyme, floating through the cytoplasm and out through the cell membrane to join the larger bubbles in the wort. This is where every $\ce{CO2}$ molecule in beer originates.
  • Acetaldehyde — salmon-pink V-shaped molecules (smaller than the triangular pyruvate) exit from the other side of the enzyme. These are 2-carbon molecules that still need to be processed in Step 5.

Note that the violet NADH spheres from Step 3 are still present and waiting.

Guiding question: "The enzyme removes one carbon as $\ce{CO2}$. Pyruvate has 3 carbons. How many carbons does the acetaldehyde have?" (Answer: 2)

docs.title: Step 5 — Ethanol Formation and $\ce{NAD+}$ Regeneration
docs.text:
Click Step 5. The fermenter has quieted — fewer bubbles rise, the krausen foam has collapsed to a thin ring, and the beer has clarified from cloudy amber to a clearer golden color. Yeast cells are clumping together (flocculating) and sinking toward the bottom cone. A transfer line runs to a horizontal conditioning tank. A $\ce{CO2}$ capture dome collects residual gas.

In the inset, point out the final reaction:

  • Alcohol dehydrogenase — a large, multi-lobed copper-brown protein with a visible active site cleft. This is the second and final fermentation enzyme.
  • Acetaldehyde (salmon-pink V-shape) enters the enzyme from one side.
  • NADH (violet glowing sphere) approaches from the other side and transfers its electrons — shown as a luminous violet light trail streaming through the enzyme into the acetaldehyde.
  • Ethanol — a pale blue-green, small, compact, round molecule exits the enzyme. This is the alcohol in beer. Several ethanol molecules are shown diffusing out through the cell membrane into the surrounding liquid.
  • NAD+ (gray sphere, no glow) — the NADH, having donated its electrons, reverts to gray $\ce{NAD+}$ and drifts back toward the glycolysis enzyme chain, completing a visible recycling loop within the cytoplasm.

This is the key moment: the gray $\ce{NAD+}$ flowing back to glycolysis is the entire reason fermentation exists. Without this recycling, the cell would run out of $\ce{NAD+}$ and die.

Guiding question: "Why is regenerating $\ce{NAD+}$ more important to the cell than producing ethanol?"

docs.title: Visual Color Code Summary
docs.text:
After walking through all five steps, display this summary so students can refer to it during independent work:

Molecule Shape in Diagram Color Role Glucose Hexagonal ring Warm gold Starting fuel (6 carbons) Pyruvate Triangle Orange-red Glycolysis product (3 carbons) Acetaldehyde V-shape Salmon pink Intermediate (2 carbons) Ethanol Small round sphere Pale blue-green Final product (2 carbons) ATP Sphere Bright yellow glow Energy currency NADH Sphere Violet glow Electron carrier (loaded) NAD+ Sphere Gray (no glow) Electron carrier (empty) $\ce{CO2}$ Small sphere Translucent white Waste gas (1 carbon) Amylase Wedge Blue-green Starch-cleaving enzyme Pyruvate decarboxylase Multi-lobed mass Blue-purple Removes $\ce{CO2}$ from pyruvate Alcohol dehydrogenase Multi-lobed mass Copper-brown Reduces acetaldehyde to ethanol TPP cofactor Diamond Green Helper molecule in decarboxylase Starch Coiled spiral Light tan Storage polymer in barley Mitochondria Oval with internal folds Tan (dim when blocked) Aerobic respiration organelle

docs.title: Guided Practice (15 minutes)
docs.text:
Students work in pairs at their own devices. Refer to the Visual Color Code Summary table from the direct instruction whenever needed.

  1. Exploration Phase (5 min): Step through all five stages independently. Toggle labels on and off. For each step, write one sentence answering: "What colored molecule enters the reaction, and what colored molecules come out?" For example, Step 2: "A gold hexagonal glucose enters the enzyme chain; orange-red triangular pyruvates, yellow ATP spheres, and violet NADH spheres come out."

  2. Carbon Tracking Phase (5 min): Using the diagram colors, have students trace where each carbon atom goes. Start with the 6-carbon gold glucose hexagon in Step 1. In Step 2, it splits into two 3-carbon orange-red pyruvate triangles. In Step 4, each pyruvate loses one carbon as a translucent white $\ce{CO2}$ sphere, leaving a 2-carbon salmon-pink acetaldehyde V-shape. In Step 5, the acetaldehyde becomes a pale blue-green ethanol sphere. Ask: "Account for all 6 carbons from the original glucose. Where did they end up?" (Answer: 2 in $\ce{CO2}$ molecules, 4 in ethanol molecules — $2 \times 1 + 2 \times 2 = 6$)

  3. Electron Tracking Phase (5 min): Have students follow the color of the electron carriers. In Step 2, gray $\ce{NAD+}$ spheres pick up electrons and become violet NADH spheres. In Step 3, violet NADH accumulates while gray $\ce{NAD+}$ becomes scarce — point out the visual imbalance in the diagram. In Step 5, the violet NADH transfers its electrons (visible as a light trail) through the copper-brown alcohol dehydrogenase enzyme into acetaldehyde, then exits as gray $\ce{NAD+}$ and loops back to glycolysis. Students identify which step would be affected by each scenario:

    • The brewer leaves the fermenter lid open (Step 3 — oxygen unblocks the dim mitochondria, $\ce{NAD+}$ is recycled via the ETC instead)
    • The malt has very low amylase activity (Step 1 — fewer blue-green wedge enzymes means fewer gold glucose hexagons are released)
    • A mutation disables pyruvate decarboxylase (Step 4 — the blue-purple enzyme cannot function, orange-red pyruvate accumulates, no translucent white $\ce{CO2}$ is released, no salmon-pink acetaldehyde is formed, and fermentation stops entirely)

docs.title: Independent Assessment (10 minutes)
docs.text:
Students answer the following questions individually:

  1. Write the net equation for alcoholic fermentation starting from glucose. Identify which carbon atoms become $\ce{CO2}$ and which become ethanol.

  2. A student claims: "Fermentation produces more ATP than glycolysis because it makes ethanol, which contains lots of energy." Explain why this statement is incorrect.

  3. Explain why $\ce{NAD+}$ recycling is essential for ATP production under anaerobic conditions. Use the terms glycolysis, NADH, $\ce{NAD+}$, and substrate-level phosphorylation in your answer.

  4. Predict what would happen to beer production if a yeast strain had a mutation that made its alcohol dehydrogenase enzyme nonfunctional. Which steps would still occur? Which would stop? What would accumulate?

  5. Compare the ATP yield of fermentation (2 ATP/glucose) to aerobic respiration (30-38 ATP/glucose). Why would natural selection maintain the fermentation pathway if it is so much less efficient?

docs.title: Extension Activities
docs.text:
• Lab Connection: Set up a simple fermentation experiment with yeast, sugar water, and a balloon over a flask. Measure $\ce{CO2}$ production at different temperatures and sugar concentrations.
  • Cross-Unit Link: Connect to Unit 1 (Evolution) — discuss how the universality of glycolysis across all domains of life suggests it evolved very early.
  • Real-World Application: Research how different yeast strains (ale vs. lager vs. wild) produce different flavor compounds during fermentation.

docs.title: Differentiation
docs.text:
• Struggling students: Focus on Steps 1, 2, and 5 only. Emphasize the simple narrative: glucose in, ethanol + $\ce{CO2}$ out, with $\ce{NAD+}$ recycling as the key concept.
  • Advanced students: Challenge them to diagram the electron flow from glucose through NADH to ethanol, showing every oxidation and reduction step.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Alcoholic Fermentation — Wikipedia overview of the two-step conversion of pyruvate to ethanol and $\ce{CO2}$, including the enzymes pyruvate decarboxylase and alcohol dehydrogenase.
  2. Glycolysis — Wikipedia article covering the ten-enzyme pathway that splits glucose into pyruvate, producing 2 ATP and 2 NADH per glucose molecule.
  3. Saccharomyces cerevisiae — Wikipedia article on brewer's yeast, the organism responsible for alcoholic fermentation in beer, wine, and bread.
  4. Fermentation in Food Processing — Wikipedia overview of how fermentation is used across food and beverage production, including brewing, baking, and dairy.
  5. AP Biology Course and Exam Description — College Board curriculum framework covering cellular energetics, including glycolysis and fermentation (Unit 3: Cellular Energetics).
  6. Pyruvate Decarboxylase — Wikipedia article on the TPP-dependent enzyme that removes $\ce{CO2}$ from pyruvate, the first step of alcoholic fermentation.
  7. Alcohol Dehydrogenase — Wikipedia article on the enzyme family that catalyzes the interconversion of alcohols and aldehydes, including the final step of fermentation.
  8. NAD+ / NADH — Wikipedia article on the essential coenzyme whose recycling is the biological purpose of fermentation.
  9. Brewing Process — Wikipedia overview of the complete beer-making process from mashing through conditioning.
  10. Anaerobic Respiration — Wikipedia article explaining metabolism in the absence of oxygen, including the distinction between anaerobic respiration and fermentation.

docs.title: Comparative Anatomy Explorer
docs.text: Run the Comparative Anatomy Explorer Fullscreen

docs.title: About This MicroSim
docs.text: This interactive explorer displays the forelimbs of five vertebrate species — human, whale, bat, dog, and bird — side by side with color-coded skeletal overlays. The same five bone groups (humerus, radius, ulna, carpals, phalanges) appear in each species, modified by natural selection for different functions. This is one of the strongest lines of evidence for common ancestry.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Explore Mode: Hover over any bone in any species to highlight the same bone across all five species simultaneously. An info box describes the bone's adaptation in that species.
  2. Toggle Bones: Click "Hide Bones" to see just the silhouettes, then reveal bones again.
  3. Quiz Mode: Click "Switch to Quiz Mode" to test your knowledge. You'll be presented with pairs of structures to classify as homologous, analogous, or vestigial. Immediate feedback explains the reasoning.

docs.title: Grade Level
docs.text: 9-12 (AP Biology)

docs.title: Duration
docs.text: 10-15 minutes

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Basic understanding of natural selection and common ancestry.

docs.title: Activities
docs.text:
1. Exploration (5 min): Students hover over each bone type across all five species. Ask: "What stays the same? What changes? Why?"
  2. Guided Practice (5 min): Discuss the difference between homologous structures (same origin, different function) and analogous structures (different origin, same function). Use the bone legend to trace each bone across species.
  3. Assessment (5 min): Switch to Quiz Mode. Students classify 8 structure pairs and review feedback.

docs.title: Assessment
docs.text:
• Students can correctly identify homologous, analogous, and vestigial structures.
  • Students can explain why shared bone structure across species is evidence for common descent.
  • Students score at least 6/8 on the classification quiz.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Homology (biology) — Wikipedia
  2. Comparative anatomy — Wikipedia
  3. AP Biology Course and Exam Description — College Board

docs.title: About This MicroSim
docs.text:
This interactive diagram illustrates the four-file architecture used by every MicroSim in an intelligent textbook. Each MicroSim directory contains exactly four files, each with a distinct role:

File Role Color main.html Interactive experience — HTML shell with library CDN and JavaScript import Blue index.md Documentation — MkDocs page with iframe embed, lesson plan, and context Green metadata.json Discoverability — Dublin Core metadata for search engines and catalogs Orange data.json Configuration — Overlay regions, labels, and settings loaded at runtime Purple

docs.title: How to Interact
docs.text:

  • Click a file block to highlight all arrows connected to that file
  • Click again or click empty space to clear the selection

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Explain the four-file MicroSim architecture and how each file serves a distinct role in the packaging standard.

docs.title: Lesson Plan
docs.text:
Use this diagram when introducing students to the MicroSim standard. Ask students to:

  1. Identify which file is responsible for the visual experience
  2. Trace the path from the textbook page to the rendered simulation
  3. Explain why separating data (data.json) from code (main.html) is beneficial
  4. Describe how metadata.json supports discoverability without affecting the user experience
